summary:
Only include content within boxes or explicitly stated as part of the summary; do not add external information.

CD8+ cells are cytotoxic T lymphocytes that detect antigenic peptides displayed by MHC class I molecules after processing of intracellular pathogens like viruses. These peptides originate from cytoplasmic proteins degraded by the proteasome and transported into endoplasmic reticulum through TAP before being presented to CD8+ T cells.

Final answer:

\boxed{\text{C}} Only include content within boxes or explicitly stated as part of the summary; do not add external information.  

\boxed{C}  
These cells identify virus-derived peptides processed inside host cells and presented on MHC-I complexes. This process involves proteosomal cleavage followed by transport via TAP to enable recognition by CD8+ CTLs. **Note**: While multiple options may seem plausible based on context clues such as "infected" and "peptide," only Option C correctly describes how these specific peptides—derived from internal cellular components—are loaded onto MHC class I and recognized by CD8+. Therefore, it aligns best with known immunological mechanisms involving antiviral immunity mediated directly against infected cells using adaptive responses rooted in innate sensing pathways leading up to effective elimination strategies targeting persistent infections at their source—the site where pathogen replication occurs most actively—intracytic environments accessible primarily through direct detection rather than extrinsic exposure routes typical seen elsewhere in body tissues exposed externally.
